questions.question: A producer emits 2 million IoT telemetry events per second that downstream teams want to replay and analyze independently. Which Azure messaging service is the correct fit?
options: Event Hubs
Service Bus topic
Event Grid custom topic
Storage Queue
questions.explanation: Event Hubs is the high-throughput partitioned, replayable log built for millions of events/sec with independent consumer groups. Service Bus is a broker for commands and tops out far below a firehose; Event Grid is a push event router with no replay; Storage Queue is a basic queue.

questions.question: Which statement correctly describes how each service handles an event once it is consumed?
options: Service Bus consumes-and-removes; Event Hubs reads-by-offset without removal; Event Grid pushes-and-forgets
All three delete the event immediately after the first successful delivery to any consumer
Event Hubs consumes-and-removes; Service Bus reads-by-offset without removal; Event Grid stores events indefinitely
Event Grid consumes-and-removes; Event Hubs pushes-and-forgets; Service Bus reads-by-offset without removal
questions.explanation: Service Bus is a broker (consume-and-remove), Event Hubs is a log where every consumer group has its own cursor and nothing is removed on read, and Event Grid pushes over HTTP and retries but stores nothing replayable long-term.

questions.question: A team wants to react to Blob Storage `BlobCreated` events in Event Grid. What kind of topic carries these, and what can the team do with it?
options: A system topic — Azure publishes to it and you can only subscribe, with no keys to publish
A custom topic — you publish the `BlobCreated` events yourself using an endpoint and key
A partner topic — a third-party SaaS publisher must forward the events to you
An event domain — you create one topic per blob container before subscribing
questions.explanation: `BlobCreated` is emitted by an Azure service, so it flows through a system topic. Only Azure publishes to system topics; you just subscribe. Custom topics are for your own app's events, and partner topics are for third-party SaaS publishers.

questions.question: Event Grid guarantees at-least-once delivery, so handlers can receive duplicate events and must be idempotent.
questions.explanation: Event Grid pushes each event and expects a 2xx within the timeout; any other response triggers retry, so duplicates can occur. Handlers must therefore be idempotent.

questions.question: What is the name of the default consumer group on an Event Hub?
options: `$Default`
`default`
`$Root`
`main`
questions.explanation: The default consumer group is named `$Default`. It is one independent cursor over all partitions of the hub.

questions.question: An Event Grid subscription's handler returns HTTP `400 Bad Request`. What does Event Grid do?
options: Skips retries and schedules the event straight for dead-lettering, because retrying will not help
Retries with exponential backoff for up to 24 hours before finally dead-lettering it
Retries immediately up to 30 times with no backoff, then drops the event silently
Returns the event to the original publisher so that it can be re-sent later
questions.explanation: A 400 (Bad Request) or 413 (Payload Too Large) is treated as a hard, non-retryable failure and goes straight to dead-lettering. Exponential backoff up to ~24h/30 attempts applies only to retryable failures.

questions.question: An Event Hub has 4 partitions. A team deploys 8 processor instances in one consumer group expecting 8x throughput. What actually happens?
options: Only 4 instances actively read (one owner per partition); the other 4 sit idle as standbys
All 8 instances read in parallel and the overall throughput roughly doubles
The hub automatically splits each partition so that all 8 instances get work
The processor throws an error because instances cannot exceed the partition count
questions.explanation: The number of partitions is the maximum number of concurrent active readers per consumer group, so only 4 instances own a partition each; the rest are idle standbys for failover. To scale parallelism you increase partitions, not instances.

questions.question: Whose responsibility is checkpointing in Event Hubs, and what does it accomplish?
options: The consumer's — it persists the last-processed offset per partition so a new processor can resume after failover
The Event Hubs service's — it automatically tracks each consumer group's offsets for you internally
The producer's — it records which partition each event was written to when it was sent
The namespace's — it deletes events that have already been read by every consumer group
questions.explanation: Event Hubs gives you offsets but does not track them; persisting 'processed up to offset N in partition P' to a durable store like Blob Storage is the consumer's job, and on restart/failover a new processor resumes from the last checkpoint.

questions.question: How do you get ordered processing of a single device's telemetry in Event Hubs?
options: Send all of that device's events with the same partition key so they land on one ordered partition
Enable a global FIFO mode on the event hub so events stay ordered across all partitions
Attach a session ID to each event the same way that Service Bus sessions work
Increase the partition count so that each device automatically gets its own partition
questions.explanation: Ordering in Event Hubs holds only within a partition. A partition key routes a logical stream (e.g. one device) to one partition, preserving per-key order; there is no global FIFO and no session concept in Event Hubs.

questions.question: Which CloudEvents v1.0 attribute corresponds to the Event Grid legacy schema's `eventType` field?
options: `type`
`source`
`subject`
`specversion`
questions.explanation: In CloudEvents, `type` is the event kind used for filtering/routing, mapping to the legacy schema's `eventType`. `source` maps to `topic`, `subject` maps to `subject`, and `specversion` maps to the version marker.

questions.question: What does Event Hubs Capture do, and how does it affect live streaming consumers?
options: Writes the stream as Avro to Blob/Data Lake on a time-or-size window, without affecting consumer throughput
Replays the stream back into the hub so that late consumers can re-read it after retention expires
Deletes events from the hub once they have been archived, thereby freeing up throughput units
Runs a custom consumer that competes with the live readers for ownership of each partition
questions.explanation: Capture is a code-free hot-path/cold-path split: it lands the same events as Apache Avro in Blob or Data Lake storage on a time or size window (whichever trips first) and does not affect live consumers' throughput.

questions.question: Migrating existing Apache Kafka producers/consumers to the Event Hubs Kafka endpoint generally requires only changing the bootstrap server and auth, with no application code changes.
questions.explanation: The Kafka endpoint makes an Event Hubs namespace wire-compatible with Apache Kafka (protocol 1.0+); an event hub maps to a Kafka topic and partitions/consumer groups map directly, so clients repoint the bootstrap server and auth without code changes. (Available on Standard and above, not Basic.)

questions.question: Consider this Event Grid webhook handler. Why is the highlighted branch necessary before Event Grid will deliver real events to a new non-Azure webhook?
options: Event Grid requires the endpoint to echo the validation code, proving it expects events and preventing abuse
It decrypts the incoming event payload using the validation code as a symmetric encryption key
It registers the webhook's TLS certificate with Event Grid for use in all future deliveries
It acknowledges each real event so that Event Grid does not attempt to retry delivering it
questions.explanation: Before delivering to a new non-Azure webhook, Event Grid sends a SubscriptionValidationEvent; the endpoint must echo the validationCode (or do the validation-URL GET) to prove it expects events. This prevents Event Grid from being used to flood arbitrary endpoints.

questions.question: Which statements about using the Azure messaging .NET SDKs with managed identity are correct? (Select all that apply.)
options: `DefaultAzureCredential` works the same across local dev (`az login`), Container Apps, App Service, and VMs
Azure SDK messaging clients are designed to be long-lived and reused, so they should be registered as pooled singletons
The SDK clients have built-in transport retries for transient broker/network errors
Every operation should construct a new client to guarantee a fresh token
Connection strings with SAS keys are the recommended default over Entra ID for production
questions.explanation: `DefaultAzureCredential` is portable across environments, the clients are long-lived singletons (register via `AddAzureClients`), and they include built-in exponential transport retries. Creating a client per operation is an anti-pattern, and Entra ID + managed identity is preferred over SAS keys/connection strings.

questions.question: An Event Grid webhook subscriber was down for an hour. After it recovers, events still trickle in slowly for a while even though the endpoint is now healthy. What is the most likely cause?
options: Adaptive delayed delivery — Event Grid backs off and delays all deliveries to a recently-failing endpoint
The 5-minute dead-letter batching delay is replaying the stored blobs back into the subscription
The publisher is being rate-limited because the subscriber's earlier 5xx responses throttled the topic
CloudEvents binary content mode buffers the events until enough of them accumulate to fill a batch
questions.explanation: Event Grid applies adaptive delayed delivery (output flow control): when an endpoint has been failing it backs off and delays all deliveries to it (up to hours) to protect the recovering endpoint. The dead-letter delay writes to blob, it does not replay into the subscription.

questions.question: A single event in an Event Hubs partition cannot be processed (poison message). What is the correct way to handle it so the partition keeps flowing?
options: Catch it, log/side-line it, and checkpoint past it in the consumer, because Event Hubs has no DLQ
Let it dead-letter to the hub's built-in DLQ sub-queue and then simply continue reading
Abandon it so that Event Hubs redelivers the event to a different consumer group instead
Rely on Event Hubs to move it to a Blob dead-letter container after the retries are exhausted
questions.explanation: Event Hubs is a log with no DLQ concept — nothing is 'rejected'. Poison handling is the consumer's job: catch, log, side-line the bad event, and checkpoint past it so it doesn't block the partition. The Blob dead-letter behavior belongs to Event Grid, and the native DLQ sub-queue belongs to Service Bus.

questions.question: Which of these statements about delivery, ordering, and dead-lettering across the three services are correct? (Select all that apply.)
options: Event Grid provides no ordering guarantee at all; events can arrive out of order
Event Hubs supports replaying history within the retention window, whereas Service Bus and Event Grid do not
Service Bus has a native DLQ, Event Grid dead-letters to Blob Storage, and Event Hubs has no dead-letter concept
All three default to exactly-once delivery, so idempotent consumers are unnecessary
Service Bus provides global FIFO across an entire queue by default
questions.explanation: Event Grid has no ordering guarantee, only Event Hubs offers replay (retention window), and the three have distinct dead-letter stories (Service Bus native DLQ, Event Grid blob, Event Hubs none). All three are at-least-once (idempotency required, not optional), and Service Bus has no global FIFO — ordering is per session.

questions.question: A `BlobCreated` event must trigger a financial charge that runs reliably, exactly-once-ish, with retry and a dead-letter path. Which composition best satisfies this?
options: Event Grid system topic → Function emits a command → Service Bus queue → worker under PeekLock with a DLQ
Event Grid custom topic → Event Hubs → Stream Analytics → Blob Storage, relying on Capture for dead-lettering
Event Hubs → Function → Event Grid, using the partition key to guarantee the charge runs exactly once
Service Bus topic → Event Grid → webhook, using adaptive delayed delivery to retry the failed charge
questions.explanation: Event Grid reacts to the discrete `BlobCreated` event, a Function validates and emits a command, and Service Bus provides the reliable single-handler processing with PeekLock, idempotency, and a native DLQ for poison messages. Event Hubs and Event Grid lack the broker semantics needed for a reliable command.
